results.possibleIdentity: Alexander Brown (often referred to as Alex Brown) was a technical director, engineer, and artist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s who was a key figure in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization. Based at Bell Telephone Laboratories in Murray Hill, New Jersey, he was listed in the E.A.T. Technical Services Directory (1969) with expertise in systems analysis, computer science, and optics. In his capacity at E.A.T., he served as Technical Director during the early 1970s and was responsible for documenting the organization's collaborative projects between artists and engineers. He is best known for compiling and editing the seminal 'Bibliography: Experiments in Art and Technology,' published by E.A.T. in 1972, which provided a comprehensive record of the intersections between the arts and physical sciences. His work was central to the activities at Automation House, E.A.T.'s New York headquarters, and he contributed to the documentation and media production of major events such as '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering.'

C. Alexander Brown was a figure associated with the experimental art scene in London, Ontario, Canada, during the 1960s. He is mentioned in the archival fonds of the influential Canadian artist Greg Curnoe (a founder of the Nihilist Spasm Band and various artist-run spaces). Records indicate that in November 1966, Brown was involved in coordinating interviews and media coverage (specifically for CTV with journalist Patrick Watson) regarding contemporary art activities and performances. His work intersected with the noise-art and multidisciplinary practices that characterized the 'London regionalism' movement, which shared thematic goals with the interdisciplinary collaborations promoted by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the same period.
